serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of tangy tofu pasta is approximately 350 g, comprising a portion of cooked pasta topped with pan-fried tofu, broccoli florets, and tomato-based sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pasta, cooked, unenriched, without added salt (FDC ID 168936) combined with Tofu, raw, firm (FDC ID 172475) and Tomato sauce, canned (FDC ID 170457) and Broccoli, cooked, boiled (FDC ID 170379); weighted composite estimate for a typical tofu pasta dish
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for tangy tofu pasta as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked pasta (~55% by weight), firm tofu (~25%), tomato sauce (~15%), and cooked broccoli (~5%), reflecting a typical restaurant-style preparation. Energy and macros are calculated proportionally. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — pasta (cooked, al dente) GI ~45; tofu has negligible GI contribution
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tangy tofu pasta as a composite dish. GI of 45 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source: cooked pasta (GI ~45, low category) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Tofu and broccoli contribute negligible glycemic load. Tomato sauce adds minor sugar but does not materially raise GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 60.2) / 100 ≈ 27.1, rounded to 27.0, based on 350 g serving with 17.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from tomato sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 350 g serving provides approximately 735 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to keep sodium within the 1500–2000 mg daily target recommended by clinical guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from pasta carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 45 and a glycemic load of ~27 per standard serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and pair this dish with additional non-starchy vegetables or a protein supplement to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and zero cholesterol from plant-based ingredients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tangy tofu pasta is a plant-based dish with low saturated fat (~1.4 g per serving) and zero dietary cholesterol, making it a heart-friendly option; however, sodium from tomato sauce should be monitored in individuals with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tangy tofu pasta is a vegetarian and vegan-friendly pasta dish served at Western-style cafes in Singapore, notably at The Tree Cafe in Funan Mall. The dish features pan-fried firm tofu cubes with a golden, slightly crispy exterior, served over a bed of cooked pasta — typically spaghetti or penne — tossed in a tangy tomato-based sauce with broccoli florets. The tomato sauce provides a bright, acidic flavour profile balanced by the mild, protein-rich tofu and the slight bitterness of broccoli. The dish is minimally processed, relying on simple pan-frying and boiling techniques. Nutritionally, it provides a moderate energy density of approximately 112 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution from tofu (~5.8 g per 100 g), low saturated fat, and zero cholesterol, making it suitable for cardiovascular health. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low category), driven by the pasta component. Sodium content is moderate at ~210 mg per 100 g, primarily from the tomato sauce. The dish is a good source of plant-based calcium from tofu (~82 mg per 100 g) and vitamin C from broccoli and tomato. It is free from dairy and meat, suitable for vegetarian, vegan, and halal diets. Clinically, it is a reasonable choice for individuals managing weight, cardiovascular risk, or blood glucose, provided sodium and portion size are controlled.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tofu pasta?
Is tofu pasta suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of tofu pasta?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for tangy tofu pasta as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for the constituent ingredients (cooked pasta, firm tofu, tomato sauce, broccoli). GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source (cooked pasta) using the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence reflects the estimation approach rather than a direct match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central ingredient entries; no single approved-source record exists for this specific dish. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for plant-based pasta dish with no animal fats or partially hydrogenated oils; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central ingredient data. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as all ingredients are plant-based (pasta, tofu, broccoli, tomato sauce); confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero for this plant-based dish; tofu is not UV-treated or fortified in this preparation; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are all estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite; no -1 sentinel values used as reasonable estimates are available from ingredient data. 7. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on cooked pasta GI from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists. 8. Cuisine tag in input was 'Coffee' (likely a GrabFood platform category artefact); corrected to 'Western-Asian Fusion' based on dish composition. 9. The dish is served at The Tree Cafe, Funan Mall, Singapore, priced at SGD 12.50. 10. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the dish is served in Singapore.
